courses.name: Chemistry
courses.objectives: The course provides an overview of general inorganic chemistry.
courses.prerequisites: Basic notions of mathematics and physics.
courses.assessment: Multiple choice questions test (30 questions in 50 minutes), minimum 18/30 to pass.
courses.references: Whitten, Davis, Peck, Stanley, CHEMISTRY, 10th Edition, Cengage Learning.

courses.name: Economics and Management
courses.objectives: The course aims to provide students with the fundamental elements of economics and management.
courses.prerequisites: Basic knowledge of mathematical concepts.
courses.assessment: Written exam with multiple-choice and open questions, theory 20 points, numerical 12 points.
courses.references: Essentials of Strategic Management, McGraw Hill; Corporate Finance, Pearson.

courses.name: Fundamentals of Computer Science
courses.objectives: Introduction to problem solving through computer programming.
courses.prerequisites: Knowledge of vectors and matrices (from Mathematics course).
courses.assessment: Practical programming assessment (Python) + oral test on theory. Min 18/30 each. Final: 3/5 practical + 2/5 theory.
courses.references: J. Hunt, A Beginners Guide to Python 3 Programming, Springer.

courses.name: General Physics
courses.objectives: Knowledge of fundamentals of classical mechanics, thermodynamics and electromagnetism.
courses.prerequisites: Precalculus. Basic calculus recommended.
courses.assessment: Two written tests (2h 30min each): 4 practical problems + 2 theoretical proofs. Min 18/30 each module.
courses.references: Physics for Scientists and Engineers, Extended Version, 6th Edition, Tipler & Mosca, Macmillan.

courses.name: Mathematics
courses.objectives: Basic mathematical tools for engineering, Mathematical Analysis and Linear Algebra.
courses.prerequisites: First/second degree equations, rational equations, trigonometry, geometry.
courses.assessment: Written test (2 hours): 4 open exercises + 4 multiple choice. Max 32 points, min 18 to pass.
courses.references: Mathematics - solved exercises and theory review by Buscema et al., Societá Editrice Esculapio.

courses.name: Physiology and Anatomy
courses.objectives: Knowledge of human body organization and physiological functions.
courses.prerequisites: Chemistry, Physics, general knowledge of Molecular Biology.
courses.assessment: Integrated oral exam (anatomy 3 questions + 1 histology, physiology open-ended). Each answer max 9 points, min 18 total.
courses.references: Gray's Anatomy for Students; Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology, 14th edition.

courses.name: Advanced Physics
courses.objectives: Advanced concepts of classical mechanics, thermodynamics, electromagnetism, quantum mechanics, statistical mechanics.
courses.prerequisites: General Physics, Calculus I.
courses.assessment: Written exam (2 hours): 3 theoretical questions (10 points each), followed by oral discussion. Min 18/30.
courses.references: Physics for Scientists and Engineers (Tipler & Mosca); Modern Physics for Scientists and Engineers (Morrison).

courses.name: Biomedical Signal Processing
courses.objectives: Principles, methods, and tools of signal processing applied to medicine and biology.
courses.prerequisites: Mathematics, Physics, Computer Science, Physiology, Statistics, Mathematics II, Electronics, Advanced Physics.
courses.assessment: Module 1 (written+oral), Modules 2&3 (written+oral), Group project. Each min 18/30. Final average of three components.
courses.references: Semmlow, Circuits, signals, and systems for bioengineers (2024); Abood, Digital Signal Processing (2020).

courses.name: Electronics and Electrotechnics
courses.objectives: Analysis of electrical systems and linear electrical networks. Basic knowledge about electronic components and devices.
courses.prerequisites: Fundamentals of Electromagnetics (Module A); Basic knowledge of electrical quantities (Module B).
courses.assessment: Module A - written numerical test (2.5 hours, 3 exercises with 6 questions). Module B - oral exam (structured 30min written + presentation + problem application + lab exercises).
courses.references: Alexander & Sadiku, Fundamentals of Electric Circuits, 6th ed; Art of Electronics (Horowitz & Hill).

courses.name: Fundamentals of Automatic Control
courses.objectives: Basic notions for analysis of systems and design of basic controllers.
courses.prerequisites: Linear algebra, calculus, basic Matlab programming.
courses.assessment: Oral discussion (about 20 minutes): 3 topics on systems theory, frequency-based control, time-domain control. Average of 3 topics for final grade.
courses.references: Astrom & Murray, Feedback systems: an introduction for scientists and engineers (2021).

courses.name: Fundamentals of Bioengineering
courses.objectives: Multidisciplinary design, development, integration, and virtual testing of bioengineering devices.
courses.prerequisites: Mathematics, Physics, Geometry, Mechanics of Solids, Electronics, Programming.
courses.assessment: Individual oral exam (50%), group presentation on labs (20%), Q&A session (30%). Min 18/31, laude >30.5.
courses.references: Kutz, Biomedical engineering and design handbook; Jerald, The VR book.

courses.name: Healthcare Information Systems and Telemedicine
courses.objectives: Concept of Healthcare as ecosystem, Telemedicine, fundamentals of computer networks, databases, and data visualization.
courses.prerequisites: Suggested to have passed Fundamentals of Computer Science.
courses.assessment: Project work (computer-based test) + oral interview. Tests evaluate design, software tools mastery, knowledge of concepts and data management.
courses.references: Kurose & Ross, Reti di calcolatori e Internet (2017); Atzeni et al., Database Systems concepts (1999).

courses.name: Healthcare Robotics
courses.objectives: Applications of robotics in biomedical field through theory and practice.
courses.prerequisites: None
courses.assessment: Oral exam: 3 questions (11 points each) on theoretical/practical aspects. Max 45 min. Min 18/30 to pass.
courses.references: Van Wynsberghe, Healthcare robots: Ethics, design and implementation (2016); Siciliano & Khatib, Handbook of Robotics (2008).

courses.name: Mathematics II
courses.objectives: Linear Algebra, differential equations, differential and integral calculus for functions of several variables.
courses.prerequisites: Mathematics I (vector spaces, differential and integral calculus).
courses.assessment: Full exam OR two partial exams. Full: 4 exercises + 2 questions (max 32, min 18). Partial 1 & 2: 4 exercises + 2 questions each (max 32, min 18 each).
courses.references: Lay, Linear Algebra and Its Applications, 4th ed; Stewart, Calculus Early Transcendentals, 7th ed.

courses.name: Measurements and Instrumentation in Biomedical Engineering
courses.objectives: Methodologies for estimating physical quantities, measurement uncertainty, measurement systems and sensors in biomedical applications.
courses.prerequisites: None (only admission requirements for degree program).
courses.assessment: Oral exam: students discuss 2 topics selected by instructor or student. Demonstration of theoretical understanding, practical application, regulatory knowledge.
courses.references: Beckwith et al., Mechanical Measurements; Figliola & Beasley, Theory and Design for Mechanical Measurements.

courses.name: Mechanics of Solids
courses.objectives: Foundations of Solid & Structural Mechanics through inductive approach.
courses.prerequisites: Linear algebra, Calculus, Mechanics and Thermodynamics (Physics I).
courses.assessment: Written test (2 hours): open/multiple-choice questions + 1 exercise. Oral test (±4 points adjustment). Grand honor (lode) based on oral preparation.
courses.references: Casini, Gizzi, Vasta, Scienza delle Costruzioni per lngegneria Biomedica (2023).

courses.name: Probability and Statistics
courses.objectives: Fundamental knowledge in probability and statistics for understanding experimental data and random phenomena.
courses.prerequisites: Linear algebra and calculus (from Mathematics I).
courses.assessment: Written exam (2 hours): 2 open exercises + 2 multiple-choice questions. Max 32 points, min 18 to pass.
courses.references: Ross, Introduction to Probability and Statistics for Engineers and Scientists, 6th ed (2021).

courses.name: Biomechanics
courses.objectives: Biomechanical analysis of human body with focus on musculoskeletal system.
courses.prerequisites: None
courses.assessment: Multiple choice questionnaire (Module MED/34, 45 min) + oral exam (Module ING/IND34). Final: 35% MED/34 + 65% ING/IND34. Min 18/30.
courses.references: Ozkaya & Nordin, Fundamentals of Biomechanics, 2nd edition, Springer.

courses.name: Biomechatronics and Biomaterials
courses.objectives: Theoretical and scientific elements on biomechatronic systems and biomaterials properties.
courses.prerequisites: Mathematics, Physics, Electronics, Chemistry.
courses.assessment: Individual written exam: 6 open-ended questions (3 per module). Evaluation of theoretical knowledge and skills application.
courses.references: Alciatore & Histand, Introduction to mechatronics and measurement systems (2012), McGraw-Hill.

courses.name: Fundamentals of Anthropology and Ethics
courses.objectives: Understanding of values, virtues, and moral characteristics relating to Engineering and biomedical sciences.
courses.prerequisites: None
courses.assessment: Written exam (30 minutes): 11 multiple-choice questions (3 points each, no penalty for wrong answers). Tests ability to apply knowledge.
courses.references: Ghilardi, Elements of Anthropology and Ethics (2025); Tambone & Ghilardi, Philosophy and Deontology of Medical Practice (2020).

courses.name: Humanities for Bioengineering
courses.objectives: Critical thinking on frontier cases of technology-life science interaction (bio-inspired robotics, CRISPR, simulation, AI).
courses.prerequisites: None
courses.assessment: Oral examination: evaluated on conceptual clarity and adequacy of case study discussion. Min 18/30 for pass, 27+ for excellent.
courses.references: Bertolaso, Artificialmente e Umanamente (2019); Jasanoff, The ethics of invention (2016); Verbeek, What things do (2021).

courses.name: Transport Phenomena and Thermodynamics
courses.objectives: Analysis, modeling and solving of problems involving transport phenomena and thermodynamics in biomedical engineering.
courses.prerequisites: None
courses.assessment: Written trial (2 hours): 2 problems on methods application. Oral trial (~1 hour): 2 practical cases. Final: 50% written + 50% oral. Min 18/30.
courses.references: Bird, Stewart & Lightfoot, Transport Phenomena 2nd Ed.; Sandler, Chemical, Biochemical and Engineering Thermodynamics (2006).

courses.name: Laboratory of Measurements
courses.objectives: Fundamental knowledge in developing and designing experiments to characterize measurement systems for biomedical applications.
courses.prerequisites: None (only admission requirements).
courses.assessment: Lab experiments + computer activities (20/30) + Design and implementation of measurement system with oral test (10/30). Cum laude for excellence.
courses.references: Beckwith et al., Mechanical Measurements; Figliola & Beasley, Theory and Design for Mechanical Measurements.

courses.name: General English
courses.objectives: Reinforces level C1 general English.
courses.prerequisites: None
courses.assessment: Level C1 written test.
courses.references: Materials provided by teachers.

courses.name: Italian
courses.objectives: Enhance knowledge of elementary Italian grammatical and syntactic structures.
courses.prerequisites: Placement test (B1+ exempted).
courses.assessment: Written test (grammar, comprehension, writing, listening) + speaking assessment during course. Pass/fail basis.
courses.references: Materials provided by teachers.

courses.name: Technical English
courses.objectives: Scientific terminology and abstract writing. Reading comprehension of medical articles.
courses.prerequisites: Must pass Year 1 test before final exam.
courses.assessment: Written test: write the abstract of a scientific article.
courses.references: Materials provided by teachers.
